contentList.content: Reset Tutorials
Welcome to Home Brew! Given the expansive scope of the changes found within the mod, it is recommended that players new to the mod reset their tutorials. Would you like to do so now?
Stealth

Attacking enemies while in Stealth grants Advantage on the Attack Roll. Be aware that creatures who have Blindsight will always detect you, no matter what.

[IE_ToggleSneak] Toggle Hide

Attacking enemies while in Stealth grants Advantage on the Attack Roll. Be aware that creatures who have Blindsight will always detect you, no matter what.

[IE_ToggleSpells] Action Radial Menu [IE_ToggleSneak] Toggle Hide

Combat in Home Brew can swing between slow and deliberate to fast and deadly, depending on how you build your characters. Combat begins by rolling Initiative to determine who acts first. All combatants are shown at the top of the screen in order of their Initiative roll, and are highlighted based on their attitude toward your party. Allies are highlighted in green, enemies in red, and neutral characters in yellow.

During any given turn, most characters will be able to utilize their Action, Bonus Action, and Reaction all within the same turn.

When building a well rounded team, it is important to consider not only your offensive measures, but also defensive. A team without any defensive capability will be quickly wiped out. Within your team, consider faciltating traditional archetypical roles - tanking, dealing damage, providing support, and controlling the opposition all lend themselves to different paths of success.

During any given turn, most characters will be able to utilize their Action, Bonus Action, and Reaction all within the same turn.

When building a well rounded team, it is important to consider not only your offensive measures, but also defensive. A team without any defensive capability will be quickly wiped out. Within your team, consider faciltating traditional archetypical roles - tanking, dealing damage, providing support, and controlling the opposition all lend themselves to different paths of success.

When rolling Initiative at the start of combat to determine turn order, party members may end up acting directly before or after each other. In these cases, character turns may be taken in any order you wish. This is also true during multiplayer.

When rolling Initiative at the start of combat to determine turn order, party members may end up acting directly before or after each other. In these cases, character turns may be taken in any order you wish. This is also true during multiplayer.

Characters will become <LSTag Type="Status" Tooltip="SURPRISED">Surprised</LSTag> when attacked while outside of combat. Surprised characters cannot act during the first round of combat, nor utilize their armours <LSTag Type="Status" Tooltip="DAMAGE_REDUCTION">Damage Reduction</LSTag>. Keep in mind that some creatures, such as Undead, are immune to mentally debilitating effects. As such, they cannot be Surprised.

Characters will become <LSTag Type="Status" Tooltip="SURPRISED">Surprised</LSTag> when attacked while outside of combat. Surprised characters cannot act during the first round of combat, nor utilize their armours <LSTag Type="Status" Tooltip="DAMAGE_REDUCTION">Damage Reduction</LSTag>. Keep in mind that some creatures, such as Undead, are immune to mentally debilitating effects. As such, they cannot be Surprised.

Taking the <LSTag Tooltip="HighGroundRules">High Ground</LSTag> provides ranged <LSTag Tooltip="AttackRoll">Attack Rolls</LSTag> with a +4 bonus. While below your target, you instead roll with a -4 penalty.

Taking the <LSTag Tooltip="HighGroundRules">High Ground</LSTag> provides ranged <LSTag Tooltip="AttackRoll">Attack Rolls</LSTag> with a +4 bonus. While below your target, you instead roll with a -4 penalty.
When you attack from above, you gain a +4 bonus to the <LSTag Tooltip="AttackRoll">Attack Roll</LSTag>. When you attack from below, you have a -4 penalty.

When a creature leaves the melee range of an enemy, their enemy may use their <LSTag Type="ActionResource" Tooltip="ReactionActionPoint">Reaction</LSTag> in order to make an <LSTag Tooltip="OpportunityAttack">Opportunity Attack</LSTag> against the creature moving away. There are several ways within Home Brew which to acquire additional Reactions. Weaponizing your Reactions can make for a potent hybrid of damage and control.

When a creature leaves the melee range of an enemy, their enemy may use their <LSTag Type="ActionResource" Tooltip="ReactionActionPoint">Reaction</LSTag> in order to make an <LSTag Tooltip="OpportunityAttack">Opportunity Attack</LSTag> against the creature moving away. There are several ways within Home Brew which to acquire additional Reactions. Weaponizing your Reactions can make for a potent hybrid of damage and control.

Using the <LSTag Type="Spell" Tooltip="Shout_Disengage">Disengage</LSTag> action allows you to leave an enemy's melee range without triggering their <LSTag Tooltip="OpportunityAttack">Opportunity Attack</LSTag>. You may also avoid Opportunity Attacks through the effects of specific passives, or other conditions. Be sure to inspect each enemy you intend to keep held down, as they may have a way to avoid your Opportunity Attacks entirely.

Using the <LSTag Type="Spell" Tooltip="Shout_Disengage">Disengage</LSTag> action allows you to leave an enemy's melee range without triggering their <LSTag Tooltip="OpportunityAttack">Opportunity Attack</LSTag>. You may also avoid Opportunity Attacks through the effects of specific passives, or other conditions. The Combat Log keeps track of your encounters - particularly the details of your battles. You may also find a record of the conversations you've had so far within your Combat Log.
The Combat Log keeps track of your encounters - particularly the details of your battles. You may also find a record of the conversations you've had so far within your Combat Log.

While dual-wielding weapons, you will always make an additional attack with your off-hand weapon when performing basic attacks. When making a <LSTag Type="Spell" Tooltip="Target_OffhandAttack">Dual-Wield Melee Attack</LSTag>, your off-hand weapon will not benefit from your <LSTag Tooltip="Strength">Global Strength</LSTag> bonus.

While dual-wielding weapons, you will always make an additional attack with your off-hand weapon when performing basic attacks. When making a <LSTag Type="Spell" Tooltip="Target_OffhandAttack">Dual-Wield Melee Attack</LSTag>, your off-hand weapon will not benefit from your <LSTag Tooltip="Strength">Global Strength</LSTag> bonus.

You can Examine creatures, objects, items, and the environment you come across during your adventures to learn more about them. Examining something tells you its statistics, features, ability scores, Resistances, Vulnerabilities, or Immunities.

Examining creatures if of vital importance within Home Brew. Many enemies you come across will have randomized subclasses, class passives, and more. Examine your enemies to learn what they are capable of and plan ahead.

Characters can spend their Reactions in one of two ways. The first, more common method, is to utilize your Reaction in response to another action or event. For example, if a character flees from you while within range of your melee weapon, you could spend a Reaction in order to make an Opportunity Attack against them.

The second method is to proactively utilize your Reaction during your turn. Within Home Brew, you will find various ways of spending your Reaction during your turn. You may choose to do so at the expense of not being able to react to events outside of your turn.

You can manage which Reactions trigger automatically, which pause the game to let you choose if you want to react, and which are ignored entirely from within in the Reactions Menu.

For example, if a character flees from you while within range of your melee weapon, you could spend a Reaction in order to make an Opportunity Attack against them.

The second method is to proactively utilize your Reaction during your turn. Within Home Brew, you will find various ways of spending your Reaction during your turn. You may choose to do so at the expense of not being able to react to events outside of your turn.

You can manage which Reactions trigger automatically, which pause the game to let you choose if you want to react, and which are ignored entirely from within in the Reactions Menu.

You can knock out a target instead of killing them, should you choose. Any type of attack or damage is capable of knocking a creature out.

Outside of playing to your characters role, you may utilize Non-Lethal Attacks strategically within combat. In Home Brew, there are many passive effects which trigger upon killing an enemy. Strategically knock your foes out in order to leave them incapacitated on the ground. When needed, you may then deliver the killing blow to trigger your passive effects.

This option can be toggled at any time in the passives tab on your hotbar.

Unless a creature has a form of Darkvision, any ranged attacks it makes while in darkness will roll with Disadvantage.

Keep in mind that there are many ways within Home Brew to either acquire Darkvision or generate light.

Indicates dark areas.

Unless a creature has a form of Darkvision, any ranged attacks it makes while in darkness will roll with Disadvantage.

Keep in mind that there are many ways within Home Brew to either acquire Darkvision or generate light.

Indicates dark areas.

Attacking a target outside of your weapon's range will cause you to roll with <LSTag Tooltip="Disadvantage">Disadvantage</LSTag>, as will making a ranged attack while <LSTag Type="Status" Tooltip="FLANKED">Threatened</LSTag> by another creature.

Attacking a target outside of your weapon's range will cause you to roll with <LSTag Tooltip="Disadvantage">Disadvantage</LSTag>, as will making a ranged attack while <LSTag Type="Status" Tooltip="FLANKED">Threatened</LSTag> by another creature.
Honour and Mythic: Difficulty Information

In Honour Mode, your foes will attempt to finish off Downed allies more often. Along with this, the most dangerous creatures in the Forgotten Realms also gain a whole host of new powers in battle known as Legendary Actions.

Home Brew expands on the amount of Legendary Actions you may come across. Be sure to examine creatures you come across in order to prepare for the battles ahead.

Mythic Mode is a difficulty entirely unique to Home Brew. In it, you will find yourself subject to a host of new dangers and debilitations. The most noticeable of differences being that of Exhaustion, Stress, and Toxicity.

Any time that you are Downed, Critically Hit, or upon starting your turn with less than 34% of your total Hit Points, you will accrue one stack of Exhaustion. While the first three stacks of Exhaustion serve only to warn you of impending consequence, four stacks or more will quickly create serious phyiscal debilitations.

Stacks of Stress are accrued at the same rate as Exhaustion, though Stress is also gained upon rolling a Critical Miss. Along with this, Stress is shared amongst the entire party - when one of your party begins to stress, the rest of the team will follow right alongside them. You may relieve both you and your party of Stress by landing killing blows or Critical Hits of your own.

Stacks of toxicity are accrued upon drinking any form of potion or elixir. For as beneficial as they are, they also have toxins within them which make it hard to digest too many in any given day. Drink too many, and you'll quickly begin to run into serious side effects.

One stack of Exhaustion, Stress, and Toxicity will always be removed upon taking a Short Rest, or by completing a combat encounter.

There are several other mechanics contained within Mythic to place pressure on the player, some that take place over time, and some that appear in sudden, horrific moments of realization. The key to success will be a well thought out strategy, finding ways to circumvent or play around your losses, and an understanding that your party will not finish this run in one piece.

You can Short Rest to recover half of your Hit Points.

All classes also recover one or more class specific resources on a Short Rest - inspect a resource's tooltip to learn more about when it is recovered.

You can Short Rest to recover half of your Hit Points.

All classes also recover one or more class specific resources on a Short Rest - inspect a resource's tooltip to learn more about when it is recovered.

When a party member reaches 0 Hit Points, they become Downed. Each turn, they make a Death Saving Throw to attempt to survive - requiring three successful Saves to live, or three failures to die.

If a character is Downed when combat ends, they will immediately regain one Hit Point.

You can revive a Downed character by using the Help action or by healing them. Throwing a healing potion at a Downed ally will not revive them.

When a party member reaches 0 Hit Points, they become Downed. Each turn, they make a Death Saving Throw to attempt to survive - requiring three successful Saves to live, or three failures to die.

If a character is Downed when combat ends, they will immediately regain one Hit Point.

You can revive a Downed character by using the Help action or by healing them. Throwing a healing potion at a Downed ally will not revive them.

After a character succeeds three <LSTag Tooltip="SavingThrow">Death Saving Throws</LSTag> while <LSTag Type="Status" Tooltip="DOWNED">Downed</LSTag>, they become Stabilised. They are no longer dying, but are still unconscious. Regaining any <LSTag Tooltip="HitPoints">Hit Points</LSTag> will cause them to wake up.

After a character succeeds three <LSTag Tooltip="SavingThrow">Death Saving Throws</LSTag> while <LSTag Type="Status" Tooltip="DOWNED">Downed</LSTag>, they become Stabilised. They are no longer dying, but are still unconscious. Regaining any <LSTag Tooltip="HitPoints">Hit Points</LSTag> will cause them to wake up.

Death is not the end for you or your party members. Using a scroll or <LSTag Tooltip="SpellSlot">Spell Slot</LSTag> to cast <LSTag Type="Spell" Tooltip="Teleportation_Revivify">Revivify</LSTag> can bring a dead character back to life with 1 <LSTag Tooltip="HitPoints">Hit Point</LSTag>. If your avatar is dead, you will not be able to <LSTag Tooltip="LongRest">Long Rest</LSTag> or move to a new area until they are revived.

Death is not the end for you or your party members. Using a scroll or <LSTag Tooltip="SpellSlot">Spell Slot</LSTag> to cast <LSTag Type="Spell" Tooltip="Teleportation_Revivify">Revivify</LSTag> can bring a dead character back to life with 1 <LSTag Tooltip="HitPoints">Hit Point</LSTag>. If your avatar is dead, you will not be able to <LSTag Tooltip="LongRest">Long Rest</LSTag> or move to a new area until they are revived.

Inspiration can be spent to reroll a failed <LSTag Tooltip="AbilityCheck">Ability Check</LSTag> or <LSTag Tooltip="SavingThrow">Saving Throw</LSTag> during dialogue. You gain Inspiration points for completing <LSTag Tooltip="BackgroundGoals">Background Goals</LSTag>. Make sure to spend these wisely, your party can only store one at a time - any additional Inspiration will be converted into Experience Points.

Inspiration can be spent to reroll a failed <LSTag Tooltip="AbilityCheck">Ability Check</LSTag> or <LSTag Tooltip="SavingThrow">Saving Throw</LSTag> during dialogue. You gain Inspiration points for completing <LSTag Tooltip="BackgroundGoals">Background Goals</LSTag>. Make sure to spend these wisely, your party can only store one at a time - any additional Inspiration will be converted into Experience Points.

Some actions require a dice roll to determine the outcome - called an Ability or Skill Check. If your roll is higher than a particular number, the Difficulty Class, you will succeed. Rolling a 1 will automatically fail a check, and rolling a 20 will automatically succeed. These rolls can be modified by Skill or Ability scores - the higher the number, the higher your roll will be. Ability scores can be increased by levelling up or by acquiring special items, and your Skills can be increased by acquiring Proficiency or Expertise in them.

Some checks will happen passively in the world, and others will be active checks performed by you during dialogue, lockpicking and trap disarmament. Active Checks let you cast Spells to enhance your roll, and Inspiration can be used to reroll if you fail a check.

Some actions require a dice roll to determine the outcome - called an Ability or Skill Check. If your roll is higher than a particular number, the Difficulty Class, you will succeed. Rolling a 1 will automatically fail a check, and rolling a 20 will automatically succeed. These rolls can be modified by Skill or Ability scores - the higher the number, the higher your roll will be. Ability scores can be increased by levelling up or by acquiring special items, and your Skills can be increased by acquiring Proficiency or Expertise in them.

Some checks will happen passively in the world, and others will be active checks performed by you during dialogue, lockpicking and trap disarmament. Active Checks let you cast Spells to enhance your roll, and Inspiration can be used to reroll if you fail a check.

Advantage lets you roll two dice for an Ability Check, Saving Throw or Attack Roll and take the higher result before adding your modifiers - making you more likely to succeed a roll. Likewise, Disadvantage makes you less likely to succeed by taking the lower result of the two rolls.

Advantage or Disadvantage can come from many different sources - enemies being Prone, characters having positive or negative attitudes towards you, and Spells that help or hinder you being only a few examples - so keep an eye out for ways to better your chances!

Indicates you have Advantage

Advantage lets you roll two dice for an Ability Check, Saving Throw or Attack Roll and take the higher result before adding your modifiers - making you more likely to succeed on a roll. Likewise, Disadvantage makes you less likely to succeed by taking the lower result of the two rolls.

Advantage or Disadvantage can come from many different sources - enemies being Prone, characters having positive or negative attitudes towards you, and Spells that help or hinder you being only a few examples - so keep an eye out for ways to better your chances!

Indicates you have Advantage

In Home Brew, all creatures are <LSTag Tooltip="Proficiency">Proficient</LSTag> with all forms of equipment. If you find a piece of equipment that you like, you can always equip and use it, provided you have enough <LSTag Type="ActionResource" Tooltip="Attunement">Attunement</LSTag> to do so.

In Home Brew, all creatures are <LSTag Tooltip="Proficiency">Proficient</LSTag> with all forms of equipment. If you find a piece of equipment that you like, you can always equip and use it, provided you have enough <LSTag Type="ActionResource" Tooltip="Attunement">Attunement</LSTag> to do so.

Sightlines mark the areas that other creatures in the world can see. You can see sightlines by toggling their visibility or Sneaking. If you move through faintly highlighted red areas you will roll a Stealth based Skill Check to see if the creature notices you - and will be discovered if you fail. Moving into heavily highlighted red areas will immediately reveal your position. If you are trying to be stealthy, these areas are worth avoiding.

[IE_ShowSneakCones] Show enemy sightlines
[IE_UISelectionDown] Hide party

Sightlines mark the areas that other creatures in the world can see. You can see sightlines by toggling their visibility or Sneaking. If you move through faintly highlighted red areas you will roll a Stealth based Skill Check to see if the creature notices you - and will be discovered if you fail. Moving into heavily highlighted red areas will immediately reveal your position. If you are trying to be stealthy, these areas are worth avoiding.

[IE_ShowSneakCones] Show enemy sightlines
Sneak

Making a successful <LSTag Type="Skills" Tooltip="Stealth">Stealth</LSTag> based <LSTag Tooltip="SkillCheck">Skill Check</LSTag> allows you to hide from watchful eyes. While hiding, you can pickpocket characters, sneak around deadly monsters, and gain <LSTag Tooltip="Advantage">Advantage</LSTag> on <LSTag Tooltip="AttackRoll">Attack Rolls</LSTag>. Suspicious characters will investigate the area you were last visible if you hide from them.

Making a successful <LSTag Type="Skills" Tooltip="Stealth">Stealth</LSTag> based <LSTag Tooltip="SkillCheck">Skill Check</LSTag> allows you to hide from watchful eyes. While hiding, you can pickpocket characters, sneak around deadly monsters, and gain <LSTag Tooltip="Advantage">Advantage</LSTag> on <LSTag Tooltip="AttackRoll">Attack Rolls</LSTag>. Suspicious characters will investigate the area you were last visible if you hide from them.

Thieves' Tools can be used to pick locks if you pass a successful <LSTag Tooltip="SleightOfHand">Sleight of Hand</LSTag> based <LSTag Tooltip="SkillCheck">Skill Check</LSTag>. If you fail the check, the Thieves' Tools will break - but worry not, every lock has a key somewhere, and if all else fails, you can simply smash your way in.

Thieves' Tools can be used to pick locks if you pass a successful <LSTag Tooltip="SleightOfHand">Sleight of Hand</LSTag> based <LSTag Tooltip="SkillCheck">Skill Check</LSTag>. If you fail the check, the Thieves' Tools will break - but worry not, every lock has a key somewhere, and if all else fails, you can simply smash your way in.

You can highlight items near you in the world to more easily spot new loot.

Press [IE_ShowWorldTooltips] in order to highlight nearby containers and items.

You can highlight items near you in the world to more easily spot new loot.

Press [IE_ShowWorldTooltips] in order to highlight nearby containers and items.
You can move most objects around in the world to discover secrets, create cover, or gain access to higher areas. Stronger characters can move heavier objects.
You can move most objects around in the world to discover secrets, create cover, or gain access to higher areas. Stronger characters can move heavier objects.
Some objects in the world are unusually tough and need to be hit with enough damage at once to actually be affected. You can always examine a tough object or enemy to see if they have any weaknesses.
Some objects in the world are unusually tough and need to be hit with enough damage at once to actually be affected. You can always examine a tough object or enemy to see if they have any weaknesses.

You can <LSTag Type="Spell" Tooltip="Projectile_Jump">Jump</LSTag> to cross chasms, navigate traps, and move over various obstacles. Your <LSTag Tooltip="Strength">Strength</LSTag> determines how far you are able to jump.

You can <LSTag Type="Spell" Tooltip="Projectile_Jump">Jump</LSTag> to cross chasms, navigate traps, and move over various obstacles. Your <LSTag Tooltip="Strength">Strength</LSTag> determines how far you are able to jump.

Hiding characters that pass a creature's line of sight must pass a Stealth based Skill Check to stay hidden - on a failure, they are revealed. Sticking to the shadows makes stealth much easier, so be sure to douse nearby light sources to create better hiding spaces. While hiding, your mouse cursor will show you how shadowed a character would be in a particular area.

Fully Visible
Lightly Obscured
Heavily Obscured

Hiding characters that pass a creature's line of sight must pass a Stealth based Skill Check to stay hidden - on a failure, they are revealed. Sticking to the shadows makes stealth much easier, so be sure to douse nearby light sources to create better hiding spaces. The icon by a character's portrait will show you how shadowed they would be in a particular area.

Fully Visible
Lightly Obscured
Heavily Obscured
Surfaces can be created by a variety of means, and some already exist in the world. Many of them can change when interacting with elements like heat or sparks, and can be washed away by water.
Surfaces can be created by a variety of means, and some already exist in the world. Many of them can change when interacting with elements like heat or sparks, and can be washed away by water.

Obscured areas are hard to explore, and fighting in the dark will cause your <LSTag Tooltip="AttackRoll">Attack Roll</LSTag> to be rolled with <LSTag Tooltip="Disadvantage">Disadvantage</LSTag> unless you have <LSTag Type="Status" Tooltip="DARKVISION">Darkvision</LSTag>. Light a torch, create fire, or cast Spells which generate light to make areas easier to navigate.

Obscured areas are hard to explore, and fighting in the dark will cause your <LSTag Tooltip="AttackRoll">Attack Roll</LSTag> to be rolled with <LSTag Tooltip="Disadvantage">Disadvantage</LSTag> unless you have <LSTag Type="Status" Tooltip="DARKVISION">Darkvision</LSTag>. Light a torch, create fire, or cast Spells which generate light to make areas easier to navigate.

Turn-Based Mode pauses the world around you, breaking down time into rounds of 6 seconds each. In Turn-Based Mode, your characters all act and move on the same turn, after which everything else in the world acts (including traps, surfaces, and other creatures). You can toggle in and out of Turn-Based Mode at any time.

Turn-Based Mode pauses the world around you, breaking down time into rounds of 6 seconds each. In Turn-Based Mode, your characters all act and move on the same turn, after which everything else in the world acts (including traps, surfaces, and other creatures). You can toggle in and out of Turn-Based Mode at any time.

No dungeon would be complete without a retinue of traps - some obvious, some hidden to all but the most <LSTag Tooltip="Perception">Perceptive</LSTag>. You can use a Trap Disarm Kit to make a <LSTag Tooltip="SleightOfHand">Sleight of Hand</LSTag> based <LSTag Tooltip="SkillCheck">Skill Check</LSTag> to neutralise these traps when they are revealed in the world. Be careful, as a failed check will break your kit and trigger the trap.

No dungeon would be complete without a retinue of traps - some obvious, some hidden to all but the most <LSTag Tooltip="Perception">Perceptive</LSTag>. You can use a Trap Disarm Kit to make a <LSTag Tooltip="SleightOfHand">Sleight of Hand</LSTag> based <LSTag Tooltip="SkillCheck">Skill Check</LSTag> to neutralise these traps when they are revealed in the world. Be careful, as a failed check will break your kit and trigger the trap.
Click to move your character somewhere in the world, or hold [IE_UILeftMouse] to dynamically move characters towards the cursor. Grouped party members will always move together.
You can rotate and move your camera to get a better view of your surroundings, or use Tactical View to see the world from a top-down perspective.
You can rotate and move your camera to get a better view of your surroundings, or use Tactical View to see the world from a top-down perspective.

Photo Mode lets you capture customised in-game photos. You can adjust the camera position, zoom, add effects and stickers, set character poses, and much more.

Enable Photo Mode by pressing [IE_UITogglePhotoMode] or clicking the camera icon beside the minimap.

Photo Mode lets you capture customised in-game photos. You can adjust the camera position, zoom, add effects and stickers, set character poses, and much more.

Enable Photo Mode by pressing [IE_UITogglePhotoMode] + [IE_UITogglePhotoModeSecondary].

Camp is a place of respite, recuperation, and reflection. You can talk to your companions about your adventure so far, and choose who will join you in future escapades.

You can also store items and take Long Rests here. Visitors may come and go from your camp throughout your adventure - be they friends or foes.

Camp is a place of respite, recuperation, and reflection. You can talk to your companions about your adventure so far, and choose who will join you in future escapades.

You can also store items and take Long Rests here. Visitors may come and go from your camp throughout your adventure - be they friends or foes.

You can end your day of adventuring and head to camp. If you have sufficient Camp Supplies, you may Long Rest by interacting with the campfire or bedrolls, which will restore all Hit Points and resources, so if your health is running low and your resources are depleted, it may be time to rest!

Visiting camp at night also allows you to talk with any companions or followers you've gathered and reflect on your relationships and adventures.

Open Rest Menu
Initiate Long Rest

You can end your day of adventuring and head to camp. If you have sufficient Camp Supplies, you may Long Rest by interacting with the campfire or bedrolls, which will restore all Hit Points and resources, so if your health is running low and your resources are depleted, it may be time to rest!

Visiting camp at night also allows you to talk with any companions or followers you've gathered and reflect on your relationships and adventures.

[IE_PanelSelect] Open Menu
Initiate Long Rest

Food and drink are needed to successfully complete a <LSTag Tooltip="LongRest">Long Rest</LSTag> at your camp and are automatically added to your Camp Supply Sack when picked up. More nutritious food and drink will have higher value when used for Long Resting.

Food and drink are needed to successfully complete a <LSTag Tooltip="LongRest">Long Rest</LSTag> at your camp and are automatically added to your Camp Supply Sack when picked up. More nutritious food and drink will have higher value when used for Long Resting.

If you try and <LSTag Tooltip="LongRest">Long Rest</LSTag> without sufficient <LSTag Tooltip="CampSupplies">Camp Supplies</LSTag>, you will not restore any resources. However, this will still progress any pending story events. You may use partial Long Rests to progress story events without expending Camp Supplies.

If you try and <LSTag Tooltip="LongRest">Long Rest</LSTag> without sufficient <LSTag Tooltip="CampSupplies">Camp Supplies</LSTag>, you will not restore any resources. However, this will still progress any pending story events. You may use partial Long Rests to progress story events without expending Camp Supplies.

Your personal Traveller's Chest is marked on the camp map, and only you can open it. Use it to store items you don't want weighing you down while exploring - you can do this quickly on your adventures through an item's Context Menu.

Your personal Traveller's Chest is marked on the camp map, and only you can open it. Use it to store items you don't want weighing you down while exploring - you can do this quickly on your adventures through an item's Context Menu.
Old man Withers is a strange wraith that commands many powers: he allows you to resurrect allies, hire help, store adventurers no longer present, and change your class. For a price, of course.
Old man Withers is a strange wraith that commands many powers: he allows you to resurrect allies, hire help, store adventurers no longer present, and change your class. For a price, of course.

Multiclassing is an advanced character progression tool that allows you to level up in multiple classes. While these combinations can be powerful, you will lose out on some higher level class features.

Recommended only for players familiar with Home Brew.

Multiclassing is an advanced character progression tool that allows you to level up in multiple classes. While these combinations can be powerful, you will lose out on some higher level class features.

Recommended only for players familiar with Home Brew.

When you gain enough experience, you can increase your character's level up to a maximum of twelve. Levelling up increases your Hit Points by 6 plus your Constitution Modifier.

At levels 5 and 9, your Proficiency Bonus is increased by 1.

At levels one, five, and nine, you receive milestone passives for your class and select a Cantrip if playing as a caster.

At levels two, six, and ten, you select two unique passives dependent on your chosen class.

At levels three, seven, and eleven, you may select any Spell, if playing a caster.

At levels four, eight, and twelve, you gain a feat.

At every odd level, you gain an additional passive tied to your chosen subclass.

Click the arrow by your portrait to level up.

At levels 5 and 9, your Proficiency Bonus is increased by 1.

At levels four, eight, and twelve, you gain a feat.

At every odd level, you gain an additional passive tied to your chosen subclass.

[IE_PanelSelect] Open Game Menu
Level Up

Some Spells and features allow strange and wonderful creatures to be summoned. They will follow their summoner around the world, but can be commanded to act separately. Summons cannot pick up items or start dialogues, and disappear when their master dies or dismisses them.

In order to have your summoned creatures act of their own accord, open the Relinquish Control container and select Relinquish Control: Summons in order to assign AI control to them during combat. To regain control of your summons, simply use that same action again to toggle it off.

Some Spells and features allow strange and wonderful creatures to be summoned. They will follow their summoner around the world, but can be commanded to act separately. Summons cannot pick up items or start dialogues, and disappear when their master dies or dismisses them.

In order to have your summoned creatures act of their own accord, open the Relinquish Control container and select Relinquish Control: Summons in order to assign AI control to them during combat. To regain control of your summons, simply use that same action again to toggle it off.

Metamagic allows sorcerers to spend <LSTag Type="ActionResource" Tooltip="SorceryPoint">Sorcery Points</LSTag> to change and enhance their Spells. When the desired Metamagic effect is toggled, all the Spells it can affect will light up - choose one of these Spells to cast the modified magic!

Metamagic allows sorcerers to spend <LSTag Type="ActionResource" Tooltip="SorceryPoint">Sorcery Points</LSTag> to change and enhance their Spells. When the desired Metamagic effect is toggled, all the Spells it can affect will light up - choose one of these Spells to cast the modified magic!

The Weave's a fickle friend - some magic has unexpected consequences. <LSTag Tooltip="WildMagic">Wild Magic</LSTag> has a 20% chance to trigger by default, unless enhanced by a passive or effect, in which case the chance increases to 35%. It may trigger from a variety of means, be it through your subclass selection, Spells you cast, or passives you select while leveling. Be careful, as these effects could be deadly or ridiculous.

It may trigger from a variety of means, be it through your subclass selection, Spells you cast, or passives you select while leveling. Be careful, as these effects could be deadly or ridiculous.

As a <LSTag Type="ActionResource" Tooltip="BonusActionPoint">Bonus Action</LSTag>, Barbarians can become enraged during combat, making them a much more formidable opponent. A Barbarian's Rage will last for three turns, however upon landing a killing blow or <LSTag Tooltip="CriticalHit">Critical Hit</LSTag>, the duration will reset.

As a <LSTag Type="ActionResource" Tooltip="BonusActionPoint">Bonus Action</LSTag>, Barbarians can become enraged during combat, making them a much more formidable opponent. A Barbarian's Rage will last for three turns, however upon landing a killing blow or <LSTag Tooltip="CriticalHit">Critical Hit</LSTag>, the duration will reset.

Bardic Inspiration can be given to allies to spend on improving an <LSTag Tooltip="AbilityCheck">Ability Check</LSTag>, boosting an <LSTag Tooltip="AttackRoll">Attack Roll</LSTag> or increasing their <LSTag Tooltip="ArmourClass">Armour Class</LSTag>. These options can be managed on the Hotbar and in the Reactions Menu.

Bardic Inspiration can be given to allies to spend on improving an <LSTag Tooltip="AbilityCheck">Ability Check</LSTag>, boosting an <LSTag Tooltip="AttackRoll">Attack Roll</LSTag> or increasing their <LSTag Tooltip="ArmourClass">Armour Class</LSTag>. These options can be managed in the Action Radial Menu and in the Reactions Menu.

Prepared Spellcasters are incredibly versatile magic users, allowing them to learn any Spell at any time. However, they must prepare different Spells when not in combat, rather than being able to cast everything they've learned at once. The number of Spells which can be prepared are equal to their level added to their main Spellcasting Ability Modifier.

[IE_PanelSelect] Open your Spellbook to prepare Spells.

Prepared Spellcasters are incredibly versatile magic users, allowing them to learn any Spell at any time. However, they must prepare different Spells when not in combat, rather than being able to cast everything they've learned at once. The number of Spells which can be prepared are equal to their level added to their main Spellcasting Ability Modifier.

[IE_ToggleSpells] Open your Spellbook to prepare Spells.

Warlocks have a limited number of <LSTag Tooltip="SpellSlot">Spell Slots</LSTag> compared to other classes, which refresh on a <LSTag Tooltip="ShortRest">Short Rest</LSTag>. Rather than having a surpluss of Spell Slots of various levels, you maintain a smaller number of much more powerful Spell Slots. A Warlock's Spells will always be cast at the highest level possible.

Warlocks have a limited number of <LSTag Tooltip="SpellSlot">Spell Slots</LSTag> compared to other classes, which refresh on a <LSTag Tooltip="ShortRest">Short Rest</LSTag>. Rather than having a surpluss of Spell Slots of various levels, you maintain a smaller number of much more powerful Spell Slots. A Warlock's Spells will always be cast at the highest level possible.

Wizards can learn some Spells from scrolls they find in the world using the scroll's Context Menu or the Spellbook. Copying these Spells into a Wizard's Spellbook requires expensive materials, represented by a gold cost. A Wizard cannot learn a Spell that is a higher level than the highest level Spell they can cast.

[IE_ContextMenu] Use a scroll's Context Menu to learn a Spell
[IE_ToggleSpells] Open Spellbook

Most characters that can cast Spells do so by spending a magical resource called a Spell Slot. These vary in power - for example, you need a first level Spell Slot to cast a less powerful first level Spell. Higher level Spells require higher level Spell Slots.

Minor magical effects can be achieved with Cantrips - which can be cast at any time without Spell Slots and increase in potency at character levels five and nine.

Most classes have some way to restore their Spell Slots depending on subclass or passive selection, and all Spell Slots are restored upon taking a Long Rest.

Most characters that can cast Spells do so by spending a magical resource called a Spell Slot. These vary in power - for example, you need a first level Spell Slot to cast a less powerful first level Spell. Higher level Spells require higher level Spell Slots.

Minor magical effects can be achieved with Cantrips - which can be cast at any time without Spell Slots and increase in potency at character levels five and nine.

Most classes have some way to restore their Spell Slots depending on subclass or passive selection, and all Spell Slots are restored upon taking a Long Rest.

Rogues can do enormous damage against enemies with their Sneak Attack, whether they loose a deadly arrow from afar or sink a blade into the unsuspecting target. However, a Sneak Attack requires that the Rogue either has Advantage on the Attack Roll or an ally within melee distance of an enemy.

You cannot Sneak Attack if you have Disadvantage on your Attack Roll. After landing a Sneak Attack, your target becomes Alert, preventing Sneak Attack from triggering on them until the end of their next turn.

At levels 5 and 9, Rogue's gain the ability to Sneak Attack an additional target.

Rogues can do enormous damage against enemies with their Sneak Attack, whether they loose a deadly arrow from afar or sink a blade into the unsuspecting target. However, a Sneak Attack requires that the Rogue either has Advantage on the Attack Roll or an ally within melee distance of an enemy.

You cannot Sneak Attack if you have Disadvantage on your Attack Roll. After landing a Sneak Attack, your target becomes Alert, preventing Sneak Attack from triggering on them until the end of their next turn.

At levels 5 and 9, Rogue's gain the ability to Sneak Attack an additional target.

Some characters will join your party as a temporary ally. They will follow a companion or avatar, denoted by whose portrait they appear next to. Selecting this follower allows you to take direct control of its movement and actions.

Followers cannot pick up items or interact in dialogues for you.

Some characters will join your party as a temporary ally. They will follow a companion or avatar, denoted by whose portrait they appear next to. Selecting this follower allows you to take direct control of its movement and actions.

Followers cannot pick up items or interact in dialogues for you.

[IE_PartyManagement] Manage party members

Some allies will stick by your side through thick and thin. Companions can join you on your adventures as part of your party, or wait in camp until you call upon them. All companions gain experience at the same time, regardless of if they fought by your side or stayed in camp. When companions are in your active party, you can control their movements and actions as if they were an avatar.

Remember - these are thinking and feeling people, with their own opinions and goals. If their attitude towards you drops low enough, they will leave - but if it is high enough, you may form a close bond with them.

All companions gain experience at the same time, regardless of if they fought by your side or stayed in camp. When companions are in your active party, you can control their movements and actions as if they were an avatar.

Remember - these are thinking and feeling people, with their own opinions and goals. If their attitude towards you drops low enough, they will leave - but if it is high enough, you may form a close bond with them.

Your party is shown on the left side of your screen as character portraits showing their health, any statuses affecting them, and if they are ready to level up. Grouped party members will move together in the world if they aren't hiding - you can ungroup party members by dragging linked portraits apart and vice versa. Characters who are far apart in the world cannot be grouped together.

If you want to make room in your party for new allies, you can ask a companion to wait at camp at any time by speaking to them.

Your party is shown on the left side of your screen as character portraits showing their health, any statuses affecting them, and if they are ready to level up. Grouped party members will move together in the world if they aren't hiding - you can ungroup party members by dragging linked portraits apart and vice versa. Characters who are far apart in the world cannot be grouped together.

If you want to make room in your party for new allies, you can ask a companion to wait at camp at any time by speaking to them.

[IE_PartyManagement] Manage party members

You can only have four companions in your party at once. If your party is full, new recruits can be sent to camp or can immediately replace a current party member.

Replaced companions can be added back to your party at any time by talking to them at camp.

You can only have four companions in your party at once. If your party is full, new recruits can be sent to camp or can immediately replace a current party member.

Replaced companions can be added back to your party at any time by talking to them at camp.

The Netherese Weave roiling within you hungers for magic items. Consume an item or face the consequences.

[IE_ToggleInventory] Open Inventory
Use the item's context menu to consume it.

The Netherese Weave roiling within you hungers for magic items. Consume an item or face the consequences.

[IE_PanelSelect] Open Inventory
[IE_ContextMenu] Use the item's context menu to consume it.

Elixirs can provide a wide range of possible benefits to a character when consumed until their next Long Rest. Drinking a new Elixir will replace any benefits granted by previously consumed Elixirs. You can craft them in the Alchemy Menu.

[IE_PanelSelect] Open the Main Menu
Craft Elixirs in the Alchemy Menu

Elixirs can provide a wide range of possible benefits to a character when consumed until their next Long Rest. Drinking a new Elixir will replace any benefits granted by previously consumed Elixirs. You can craft them in the Alchemy Menu.

Press [IE_UIToggleAlchemy] to open the Alchemy Menu and craft Elixirs.

You can pickpocket items from characters while hiding. While next to but hidden from a character, you can use the Context Menu to pickpocket, and then select which items you want to steal. You will have to succeed a <LSTag Tooltip="SleightOfHand">Sleight of Hand</LSTag> based <LSTag Tooltip="SkillCheck">Skill Check</LSTag> to successfully steal the items - more valuable items will make the check more difficult. Failing the check will mean you are noticed, and branded a criminal.

You can pickpocket items from characters while hiding. While next to but hidden from a character, you can use the Context Menu to pickpocket, and then select which items you want to steal. You will have to succeed a <LSTag Tooltip="SleightOfHand">Sleight of Hand</LSTag> based <LSTag Tooltip="SkillCheck">Skill Check</LSTag> to successfully steal the items - more valuable items will make the check more difficult. Failing the check will mean you are noticed, and branded a criminal.

Remember: your actions most definitely have consequences! Taking items highlighted red will count as a crime, as will acts of violence. If you're causing trouble, characters may call their associated guards on you - leading to fines, jail time, or possibly worse.

Remember: your actions most definitely have consequences! Taking items highlighted red will count as a crime, as will acts of violence. If you're causing trouble, characters may call their associated guards on you - leading to fines, jail time, or possibly worse.

When an illithid parasite infects a mind, it feeds and fattens itself upon the individual's psionic potential. If you kill an infected individual, you can take their parasite and consume its stored psychic energy to unlock new powers.

After consuming your first parasite, you will automatically absorb the psionic potential of future illithid specimens. The psionic essence absorbed from these tadpoles is shared across your party.

Warning: Each parasite consumed will permanently lower your Hit Points by 6. There is no way to reverse this!

[IE_ToggleInventory] Open Inventory
Consume Tadpole

When an illithid parasite infects a mind, it feeds and fattens itself upon the individual's psionic potential. If you kill an infected individual, you can take their parasite and consume its stored psychic energy to unlock new powers.

After consuming your first parasite, you will automatically absorb the psionic potential of future illithid specimens. The psionic essence absorbed from these tadpoles is shared across your party.

Warning: Each parasite consumed will permanently lower your Hit Points by 6. There is no way to reverse this!

[IE_PanelSelect] Open Inventory
Consume Tadpole

Consuming the psychic potential of illithid parasites grants you new powers - be they active abilities or passive enhancements. When you consume a tadpole, you can use it to unlock a new feature in the Illithid Powers Menu.

Companions can also learn illithid powers, but may have mixed feelings about meddling in mind flayer magic, but could be convinced.

Warning: Each parasite consumed will permanently lower your Hit Points by 6. There is no way to reverse this!

[IE_PanelSelect] Open Game Menu
Open Illithid Powers Menu

Warning: Each parasite consumed will permanently lower your Hit Points by 6. There is no way to reverse this!

The Context Menu allows you to use items, tag them as wares, or send them to other party members. Some items or objects in the world have special interactions such as lockpicking or climbing that can be found in the Context Menu.

The Context Menu allows you to use items, tag them as wares, or send them to other party members. Some items or objects in the world have special interactions such as lockpicking or climbing that can be found in the Context Menu.

The Party View allows you to manage character inventories, Spells, and reactions. Equipment can also be managed here, letting you handle encumbrance, split stacks of items, search for specific items, and mark wares. The Party View also lets you compare the character sheets of party members and examine class features.

[IE_PanelSelect] Open Game Menu
Open Character Sheet

The Party View also lets you compare the character sheets of party members and examine class features.

[IE_TogglePartyCharacterView] Open Party View

If you pick up too many heavy items, you will become Encumbered.

Encumbered characters move much more slowly, cannot jump or climb, and roll with Disadvantage on Ability Checks and Attack Rolls, as well as on Strength, Dexterity, and Constitution based Saving Throws. Drop excess items to become unencumbered.

If you pick up too many heavy items, you will become Encumbered.

Encumbered characters move much more slowly, cannot jump or climb, and roll with Disadvantage on Ability Checks and Attack Rolls, as well as on Strength, Dexterity, and Constitution based Saving Throws. Drop excess items to become unencumbered.
You can remove Dye colouring your equipment with a Dye Remover. This item can be purchased from traders.
You can remove Dye colouring your equipment with a Dye Remover. This item can be purchased from traders.

The Journal records your adventure so far - who you've helped, where you've been, and where you might need to go next. If a goal points you to a specific location, it will appear as a marker on your map. You can also view every conversation you've had so far in the Dialogue History tab.

[IE_PanelSelect] Open Journal

The Journal records your adventure so far - who you've helped, where you've been, and where you might need to go next. If a goal points you to a specific location, it will appear as a marker on your map. You can also view every conversation you've had so far in the Dialogue History tab.

[IE_ToggleJournal] Open Journal

Specific locations across Faerûn are marked with glowing transportation sigils - Waypoints. Canny adventurers can use these Waypoints to immediately travel to locations they've been to before.

[IE_ToggleMap] Open Map

Specific locations across Faerûn are marked with glowing transportation sigils - Waypoints. Canny adventurers can use these Waypoints to immediately travel to locations they've been to before.

[IE_PanelSelect] Open Game Menu
Open Map

The Map shows the areas you've explored along your adventure. As you travel further afield, more of the world will be shown on the Map. You can use it to view quest locations, custom markers, and instantly travel between Waypoints that have been discovered in the world.

Characters in the world also appear on the map as coloured circles: green allies, red enemies, and yellow neutral parties. Small arrows near these circles indicate if the character is below or above you.
While travelling, a minimap will be shown in the corner of the screen - you can change how the minimap works in the Options Menu.

[IE_PanelSelect] Open Game Menu
Open Map

Characters in the world also appear on the map as coloured circles: green allies, red enemies, and yellow neutral parties. Small arrows near these circles indicate if the character is below or above you.
While travelling, a minimap will be shown in the corner of the screen - you can change how the minimap works in the Options Menu.

[IE_ToggleMap] Open Map

You can bring your friend's avatar back to the party by using Withers' Wardrobe of Wayward Friends in your camp. They'll join as long as there's a free spot in your party. You cannot do this at night.

You can bring your friend's avatar back to the party by using Withers' Wardrobe of Wayward Friends in your camp. They'll join as long as there's a free spot in your party. You cannot do this at night.

Your friend's avatar will remain in your party while they're offline. You can dismiss them by talking to them and selecting the option to wait in camp. Certain limitations apply. You can only dismiss custom avatars, not origin characters, and only when a camp is reachable.

You can bring avatars back by interacting with Withers' Wardrobe of Wayward Friends in camp. If any player joins your game later, they will be assigned a dismissed avatar if there is any. Only when there is no unclaimed avatar will they be able to create a new one.

Your friend's avatar will remain in your party while they're offline. You can dismiss them by talking to them and selecting the option to wait in camp. Certain limitations apply. You can only dismiss custom avatars, not origin characters, and only when a camp is reachable.

You can bring avatars back by interacting with Withers' Wardrobe of Wayward Friends in camp. If any player joins your game later, they will be assigned a dismissed avatar if there is any. Only when there is no unclaimed avatar will they be able to create a new one.

You can Ping objects, locations, and creatures to highlight it for yourself and other players.

[IE_Ping] Ping

In multiplayer sessions, you can listen into another player's conversation by clicking on the characters engaged in it. While listening in this way, you can select dialogue options to suggest it to the player speaking.

In multiplayer sessions, you can listen into another player's conversation by clicking on the characters engaged in it. While listening in this way, you can select dialogue options to suggest it to the player speaking.

Many people are willing to trade with you - but how good a deal you get depends on how well-liked you are.

You can trade to quickly buy and sell items, or haggle to get a better value - haggling in a trader's favour will make them like you more and get you better prices in future.

Many people are willing to trade with you - but how good a deal you get depends on how well-liked you are.

You can trade to quickly buy and sell items, or haggle to get a better value - haggling in a trader's favour will make them like you more and get you better prices in future.
No God
You worship no god, or do not consider yourself to be strongly bound to any particular belief.
Personality
Select an additional personality type. Personality type affect your available dialogue choices.
None
You have no additional personality beyond the obvious.
Hot-Blooded
You are impulsive, direct, and prone to explosive bouts of rage.
Silver-Tongued
You are charming and persuasive, always ready with a timely quip.
Devout
You are steadfast in your faith, guided by conviction, and interpret events through a spiritual lens.
Harmonious
You are in tune with the rhythms of nature, seeing the world through cycles of growth and decay.
Disciplined
You are unshakable, determined to see things through no matter the odds.
Contemplative
Calm by nature, you search for inner order even in the most chaotic moments.
Pragmatic
You are perceptive and cautious, always assessing situations with clear, practical judgment.
Quick-Witted
You are clever and sly, always fishing for opportunities hidden between the lines.
Unbridled
You are emotionally volatile, your personality shaped by the raw power that courses through you.
Bound
Your motivations are shaped, subtly or overtly, by promises or deals you’ve made in the past.
Knowledgeable
You are analytical to the point of obsession, dissecting problems in pursuit of deeper truths.

Character Trait
Select an additional character trait. Character traits provide alternate ways to craft your character, and may provide both positive or negative effects.
None
You have no additional character traits.
Adrenaline Surge

While below 50% of your maximum <LSTag Tooltip="HitPoints">Hit Points</LSTag>, you gain a +2 bonus to all damage dealt. However, while above 50% of your maximum Hit Points, you receive a -2 penalty instead.
Blind Emotions
You permanently benefit from the effects of being <LSTag Type="Status" Tooltip="CALM_EMOTIONS">Calmed</LSTag>. However, your targeting range is limited to [1].
Bloody Results

Gain a bonus to damage dealt equal to your <LSTag Tooltip="ProficiencyBonus">Proficiency Bonus</LSTag>. However, when dealing damage, you take damage equal to your Proficiency Bonus multiplied by two.
Borrowed Time

At the beginning of any combat encounter, you receive <LSTag Tooltip="TemporaryHitPoints">Temporary Hit Points</LSTag> equal to your level multiplied by two. Upon ending combat, you take Force damage equal to your level multiplied by four.
Breathe Deep

Gain the ability to center yourself with a deep breath, restoring your <LSTag Type="ActionResource" Tooltip="ActionPoint">Action</LSTag>. At the end of your turn, you immediately fall <LSTag Type="Status" Tooltip="UNCONSCIOUS">Unconscious</LSTag>, causing you to lose your next turn.
Breathe Deep

Center yourself with a deep breath, restoring your <LSTag Type="ActionResource" Tooltip="ActionPoint">Action</LSTag>. At the end of your turn, you will immediately fall <LSTag Type="Status" Tooltip="UNCONSCIOUS">Unconscious</LSTag>, causing you to lose your next turn.
Confidently Incorrect
Upon casting a Spell, there is a 10% chance that it will erupt in a violent explosion, damaging everything within [1] of your target.
Critical Loss

Reduce the number needed to roll a <LSTag Tooltip="CriticalHit">Critical Hit</LSTag> by 2 when making a weapon based <LSTag Tooltip="AttackRoll">Attack Roll</LSTag>. However, the damage of your Critical Hits is permanently halved.
Crybaby
Upon being damaged while not enraged, you immediately fall <LSTag Type="Status" Tooltip="PRONE">Prone</LSTag> and begin to cry. Upon starting your next turn, you become enraged for three turns.
Crying
Affected creature is crying. Upon expiration of this status, they will throw a tantrum and become enraged for three turns!
Delusions in the Dark

Gain <LSTag Tooltip="Resistant">Resistance</LSTag> to Necrotic damage and status immunity to Fear-based effects. However, you also become <LSTag Tooltip="Vulnerable">Vulnerable</LSTag> to Radiant damage.
Gambler
Upon starting your turn in combat, you have a 10% chance to only roll <LSTag Tooltip="CriticalHit">Critical Hits</LSTag>. However, you also have a 10% chance to lose your turn entirely.
Gambler: Success!
Affected creature will only roll <LSTag Tooltip="CriticalHit">Critical Hits</LSTag> until the end of their current turn.
Gambler: Failure!
Affected creature is paralyzed, unable to act. Attacks made against them will always result in a <LSTag Tooltip="CriticalHit">Critical Hit</LSTag>.
Gentle Giant
You are permanently <LSTag Type="Status" Tooltip="ENLARGE_BARBARIAN">Enlarged</LSTag>. However, rather than having your damage increased by [1], it is instead reduced.
Gilded Pilfer
When dealing damage, you deal an additional point of damage for every 100 gold pieces your target has in their inventory, consuming them in the process.
Glass Cannon

Gain an additional <LSTag Type="ActionResource" Tooltip="ActionPoint">Action</LSTag> and <LSTag Type="ActionResource" Tooltip="BonusActionPoint">Bonus Action</LSTag>. However, you also receive a -10 penalty to your <LSTag Tooltip="ArmourClass">Armour Class</LSTag>, your maximum <LSTag Tooltip="HitPoints">Hit Points</LSTag> are halved, and you make all <LSTag Tooltip="SavingThrow">Saving Throws</LSTag> with <LSTag Tooltip="Disadvantage">Disadvantage</LSTag>.
Hands of Calamity
Upon killing an enemy with a melee-based <LSTag Tooltip="AttackRoll">Attack Roll</LSTag>, everything within [1] of them will receive [2].
Hasty Reflexes

Gain an additional <LSTag Type="ActionResource" Tooltip="ReactionActionPoint">Reaction</LSTag>. When making an <LSTag Tooltip="AttackRoll">Attack Roll</LSTag> which uses a Reaction, you roll with <LSTag Tooltip="Disadvantage">Disadvantage</LSTag>.
Haymaker
Gain a +5 bonus to all <LSTag Tooltip="AttackRoll">Attack Rolls</LSTag>. However, you are unable to roll <LSTag Tooltip="CriticalHit">Critical Hits</LSTag>.
Healthy Chills

When being healed, you always recover the maximum amount of <LSTag Tooltip="HitPoints">Hit Points</LSTag> possible. However, upon being healed, you also become <LSTag Type="Status" Tooltip="CHILL_TOUCH">Bone Chilled</LSTag> for three turns.
Heavy Handed

Melee-based <LSTag Tooltip="AttackRoll">Attack Rolls</LSTag> will never deal less than 8 damage. However, you make all melee-based <LSTag Tooltip="AttackRoll">Attack Rolls</LSTag> with <LSTag Tooltip="Disadvantage">Disadvantage</LSTag>.
Hyperfixation

You have <LSTag Tooltip="Advantage">Advantage</LSTag> on all <LSTag Tooltip="Concentration">Concentration</LSTag> based <LSTag Tooltip="SavingThrow">Saving Throws</LSTag>. However, upon starting your turn while not Concentrating, you have a 10% chance to become <LSTag Type="Status" Tooltip="STUNNED">Stunned</LSTag> and lose your turn.
Ironbourne
You are considered to be a Construct, and as such, gain status based immunity to a number of conditions. However, during combat, you cannot be healed.
Immovable Boulder
You are unable to be forcibly moved or fall <LSTag Type="Status" Tooltip="PRONE">Prone</LSTag>. However, you have a -10 penalty to your Initiative.
Lefty

Gain the ability to make an off-hand weapon attack as a <LSTag Type="ActionResource" Tooltip="BonusActionPoint">Bonus Action</LSTag>, and all off-hand based <LSTag Tooltip="AttackRoll">Attack Rolls</LSTag> are rolled with <LSTag Tooltip="Advantage">Advantage</LSTag>. However, all Attack Rolls made with your main-hand are rolled with <LSTag Tooltip="Disadvantage">Disadvantage</LSTag>.
Let Me Solo Them

Reduce the number needed to roll a Critical Hit by 4, and your Hit Points are doubled. However, you are unable to utilize Attunement.

Warning: Starting your turn or combat while utilizing your Attunement will instantly kill you.
Sudden Death!
Affected creature has died!
Lone Wolf

While traveling with just one other companion, you gain an additional Action, Bonus Action, Reaction, your maximum Hit Points are doubled, and you gain 12 additional points of Attunement.

While traveling completely alone, you gain three additional Actions, Bonus Actions, Reactions, your Hit Points are quadrupled, and you gain 24 additional points of Attunement.
Narcoleptic

Upon starting your turn, you have a 33% chance to fall <LSTag Type="Status" Tooltip="SLEEP">Asleep</LSTag>. Upon waking from a sleep-based Condition, you receive <LSTag Tooltip="TemporaryHitPoints">Temporary Hit Points</LSTag> equal to your level multiplied by 4.
Natural Medium

Permanently gain the effects of <LSTag Type="Spell" Tooltip="Shout_DetectThoughts">Detect Thoughts</LSTag>, <LSTag Type="Spell" Tooltip="Shout_SpeakWithAnimals">Speak with Animals</LSTag>, and <LSTag Type="Spell" Tooltip="Target_SpeakWithDead">Speak with Dead</LSTag>. However, you make all Charisma-based <LSTag Tooltip="AbilityCheck">Ability Checks</LSTag> with <LSTag Tooltip="Disadvantage">Disadvantage</LSTag>.
Overcharged

When dealing damage with Spells, you deal additional damage equal to your <LSTag Tooltip="ProficiencyBonus">Proficiency Bonus</LSTag>. However, you also have <LSTag Tooltip="Disadvantage">Disadvantage</LSTag> on all Constitution-based <LSTag Tooltip="SavingThrow">Saving Throws</LSTag>.
Overly Difficult

Spells you cast increase their <LSTag Tooltip="DifficultyClass">Difficulty Class</LSTag> by two. However, you make all <LSTag Tooltip="SavingThrow">Saving Throws</LSTag> with <LSTag Tooltip="Disadvantage">Disadvantage</LSTag>.
Packing
Double your <LSTag Tooltip="CarryWeight">Carrying Capacity</LSTag>. However, each turn you have a 5% chance to become <LSTag Type="Status" Tooltip="OFF_BALANCED">Off Balanced</LSTag>.
Planar Skipping

Each turn, you may teleport up to [1] as a free action. However, you also have a 33% chance upon doing so to be suddenly <LSTag Type="Status" Tooltip="BANISHED_SPELL">Banished</LSTag> when doing so, and your <LSTag Tooltip="MovementSpeed">Movement Speed</LSTag> is reduced by [2].
Planar Skip
Teleport up to [1] as a free action. However, you also have a 33% chance to be suddenly <LSTag Type="Status" Tooltip="BANISHED_SPELL">Banished</LSTag>.
Reactive Loss

You no longer provoke <LSTag Tooltip="OpportunityAttack">Opportunity Attacks</LSTag>. However, you are unable to utilize your <LSTag Type="ActionResource" Tooltip="ReactionActionPoint">Reactions</LSTag> during combat.
Reckless

Gain <LSTag Tooltip="Advantage">Advantage</LSTag> on all <LSTag Tooltip="AttackRoll">Attack Rolls</LSTag> and damage rolls. However, you also become <LSTag Tooltip="Vulnerable">Vulnerable</LSTag> to all damage.
Slow Roller

Your <LSTag Tooltip="MovementSpeed">Movement Speed</LSTag> is halved. However, upon damaging an enemy with a melee-based <LSTag Tooltip="AttackRoll">Attack Roll</LSTag>, you halve their Movement Speed as well.
Slow Roller
Affected creature's <LSTag Tooltip="MovementSpeed">Movement Speed</LSTag> is halved.
Stinky

You gain status-based immunity to <LSTag Type="Status" Tooltip="BIBBERBANG_FUMES">Noxious Fumes</LSTag>. However, while moving you will create a [1] cloud of Noxious Gas and you make all Charisma-based <LSTag Tooltip="AbilityCheck">Ability Checks</LSTag> with <LSTag Tooltip="Disadvantage">Disadvantage</LSTag>.
Split Focus
Gain the ability to target two enemies at once with your weapon-based <LSTag Tooltip="AttackRoll">Attack Rolls</LSTag>. However, all weapon-based Attack Rolls also deal half damage.
Sunlight Aversion

While in an obscured area, you gain <LSTag Tooltip="Advantage">Advantage</LSTag> on all <LSTag Tooltip="AttackRoll">Attack Rolls</LSTag>. However, while unobscured, you roll with <LSTag Tooltip="Disadvantage">Disadvantage</LSTag> instead.
Sunlight Aversion: Obscured
Affected creature has <LSTag Tooltip="Advantage">Advantage</LSTag> on all <LSTag Tooltip="AttackRoll">Attack Rolls</LSTag>.
Sunlight Aversion: Unobscured
Affected creature has <LSTag Tooltip="Disadvantage">Disadvantage</LSTag> on all <LSTag Tooltip="AttackRoll">Attack Rolls</LSTag>.
Unhinged

While in combat, killing another creature will immediately restore [1]. However, if you fail to kill a creature within three turns, you become stricken with <LSTag Type="Status" Tooltip="MADNESS">Madness</LSTag> either until you do, or until three turns have passed.
Unhinged

Affected creature is slowly being driven to <LSTag Type="Status" Tooltip="MADNESS">Madness</LSTag>. Should they fail to land a killing blow before this condition expires, they will become stricken with Madness either until they do, or until three turns have passed.
Unstable Source

Each turn in combat, you have a 10% chance to experience a positive surge of <LSTag Tooltip="WildMagic">Wild Magic</LSTag>. However, upon recovering from being <LSTag Type="Status" Tooltip="DOWNED">Downed</LSTag>, you have a 100% chance to experience a negative surge.
Waning Immortality

Your maximum <LSTag Tooltip="HitPoints">Hit Points</LSTag> are doubled. However, each time that you go down, your maximum Hit Points are permanently reduced by 4. This reduction in Hit Points cannot be undone.
Waning Immortality
Your maximum <LSTag Tooltip="HitPoints">Hit Points</LSTag> have been reduced by 4 per stack of this condition. This reduction in Hit Points cannot be undone.
Wretch

You make all Attack Rolls, Ability Checks, and Saving Throws with Disadvantage.

Warning: Taking this trait will drastically impact intended game balance.
Class Archetype
Select a class archetype. Class archetypes are powerful effects which immediately enable numerous playstyles, allowing you to craft builds in unique and various ways.
None
You have no class archetype.
Arcane Precision
Reduce the number needed to roll a <LSTag Tooltip="CriticalHit">Critical Hit</LSTag> by 2 when making a Spell based <LSTag Tooltip="AttackRoll">Attack Roll</LSTag>.
Combat Mastery
Gain a +4 bonus to your <LSTag Tooltip="AttackRoll">Attack Rolls</LSTag>.
Deepened Resolve
Gain a bonus to your <LSTag Tooltip="SavingThrow">Saving Throws</LSTag> equal to your <LSTag Tooltip="ProficiencyBonus">Proficiency Bonus</LSTag>.
Imposing Will
Gain a +2 bonus to your <LSTag Tooltip="DifficultyClass">Difficulty Class</LSTag> when casting Spells or applying conditions.
Iron Breaker
When dealing damage, you ignore all physical <LSTag Tooltip="Resistant">Resistances</LSTag>.
Martial Precision
Reduce the number needed to roll a <LSTag Tooltip="CriticalHit">Critical Hit</LSTag> by 2 when making a weapon based <LSTag Tooltip="AttackRoll">Attack Roll</LSTag>.
Restorative Touch
When healing yourself or another creature, restore an additional 4 <LSTag Tooltip="HitPoints">Hit Points</LSTag>.
Tempered Body
Gain <LSTag Tooltip="Resistant">Resistance</LSTag> to all physical damage.
Tempered Mind
Gain <LSTag Tooltip="Resistant">Resistance</LSTag> to all elemental damage.
Weave Breaker
When dealing damage, you ignore all elemental <LSTag Tooltip="Resistant">Resistances</LSTag>.
Relinquish Control
Relinquish control of one or more party members or summons, allowing them to act of their own accord.
Relinquish Control: Melee
Relinquish control of one or more party members, allowing them to act of their own accord while in combat. They will attempt to target enemies from up close.
Relinquished Control: Melee
Affected creature has had their control relinquished. They will attempt to target enemies from up close while in combat.
Relinquish Control: Melee Support
Relinquish control of one or more party members, allowing them to act of their own accord while in combat. They will attempt to target enemies from up close, though will focus primarily on support.
Relinquished Control: Melee Support
Affected creature has had their control relinquished. They will attempt to target enemies from up close in combat, though will focus primarily on support.
Relinquish Control: Ranged
Relinquish control of one or more party members, allowing them to act of their own accord while in combat. They will attempt to target enemies from a distance.
Relinquished Control: Ranged
Affected creature has had their control relinquished. They will attempt to target enemies from a distance while in combat.
Relinquish Control: Ranged Support
Relinquish control of one or more party members, allowing them to act of their own accord while in combat. They will attempt to target enemies from a distance while focusing primarily on support.
Relinquished Control: Ranged Support
Affected creature has had their control relinquished. They will attempt to target enemies from a distance while in combat though focus primarily on support.
Relinquish Control: Summons

Relinquish control of your summoned creatures passively, allowing them to act of their own accord while in combat based on their equipment and abilities.

Casting this ability again will toggle the effect.
Relinquished Control: Summoner
Affected creature's summons will act of their own accord while in combat based on their equipment and abilities.
Relinquished Control: Summon
Affected creature has had their control relinquished. They will attempt to target enemies while in combat based on their equipment and abilities.
Relinquish Control: Recovery

Cause creatures who you have relinquished control over to regain their resources at the end each combat encounter.

Casting this ability again will toggle the effect.
Relinquish Control: Recovery
Affected creature will regain their resources at the end each combat encounter.
Regain Control
Regain control of one or more party members.
Action
Actions are your primary resource in combat.
Bonus Action
Bonus Actions are your secondary resource in combat.
Reaction
Reactions are responses you can have to events which can trigger both during and outside of your turn.

Aether Suspension

While enabled, this character will not participate in combat encounters. All non-participants vanish at the beginning of combat, only reappearing once combat has ended.

Should all other party memberes flee combat, this character will have three turns to leave combat as well. After three turns, if they are still in combat, they will immediately die.

If all members of the party have either been defeated or are non-participants, the game will immediately end.
Aether Suspension: Final Stand

While enabled, this character will not initially participate in combat encounters. All non-participants vanish at the beginning of combat, only reappearing once combat has ended.

Once all allied active combatants have been killed, Downed, have fled combat, or are under the effects of Aether Suspension, this character will rejoin the combat encounter.
Aether Suspension
Affected creature will not participate in this combat encounter.
Aether Suspension: Final Stand

Affected creature will not participate in this combat encounter until all allied active combatants have been killed, <LSTag Type="Status" Tooltip="DOWNED">Downed</LSTag>, have fled combat, or are under the effects of <LSTag Type="Status" Tooltip="BANISHED">Aether Suspension</LSTag>.
Final Stand
Affected creature is the last line of defense! It's now or never!
Incapacitated
Affected creature is unable to act, and will remain <LSTag Type="Status" Tooltip="DOWNED">Downed</LSTag> until the end of the current combat encounter.
Left for Dead
Affected creature will die if combat has not ended when this condition expires.
